181.title: Aladdin and the Magic Lamp
181.text:
Aladdin was not, by any honest measure, a model child.
He was supposed to be learning his father's trade. He was not doing that. He was supposed to be helping his mother with her spinning. He was not doing that either. He was, at any given moment, somewhere in the streets of the city playing games with other boys and generally making the most of a life that asked very little of him.
His mother sighed a great deal.
Then one afternoon, a stranger appeared in the marketplace.
He was tall, with a dark beard and fine robes and the particular smile of someone who has already decided they like you very much. He found Aladdin in the street and looked at him with warm, delighted eyes.
"Are you the son of Mustapha the tailor?" he asked.
Aladdin said he was.
"Then I am your uncle!" the man declared, pressing gold coins into Aladdin's hand. "I have been traveling in far countries for many years. Come — let us go to your mother. I have missed you all terribly."
Aladdin had no memory of any uncle. But the coins were real, and the man was convincing, and Aladdin led him home.
The man was not an uncle. He was a sorcerer from Africa, and he had traveled thousands of miles because his magic books had told him about a lamp — an extraordinary lamp buried in an enchanted cave — and also told him one important thing: only an innocent young boy could go in and get it. He had found his boy.
The next morning, the sorcerer took Aladdin out of the city and into the hills. When Aladdin asked where they were going, the sorcerer said, "Just a little further. Something remarkable." This is what adults say when they don't want to answer the question.
They came to a narrow valley. The sorcerer lit a fire, threw in a powder, said words in a language Aladdin didn't recognize, and the ground shook. A flat stone appeared with a brass ring set into it.
"Lift it," the sorcerer said. "Only you can."
Aladdin pulled. The stone rose smoothly, revealing steps going down into darkness.
"Go down," the sorcerer said. "Three halls. Don't touch the jewels on the walls — they'll burn you. Find an old lamp on a shelf. Bring only that."
He pressed a ring onto Aladdin's finger. "For protection. Now go."
The cave was extraordinary — walls embedded with rubies and emeralds glowing with their own soft light. Aladdin kept his hands at his sides through all three halls, found the lamp exactly where promised, and started back.
At the bottom of the stairs, he paused.
The lamp wasn't heavy. His robe had room. He looked at the walls. Surely these jewels weren't what the sorcerer wanted. Surely a handful of them could change his mother's life.
He filled his pockets. His belt. Every fold of his robe until he was bristling with gems. Then he climbed toward the light.
"Give me the lamp!" the sorcerer called down, reaching his hands in before Aladdin was even at the top. He needed the lamp before anything else, before Aladdin could ask questions.
That desperation was a mistake. Aladdin noticed it.
"Help me up first," Aladdin said, "and then I'll give you the lamp."
"Give me the lamp first."
"Help me up first."
The sorcerer, furious, said words. The stone slammed shut. Aladdin, in the dark, understood that he had just learned something important about the sorcerer's intentions.
He sat in the dark for a while. Then, rubbing his hands together for warmth, he accidentally rubbed the ring on his finger.
Smoke poured out. A genie appeared — not enormous, a ring-genie, smaller and more manageable — and asked what Aladdin wished.
"Get me out of here," Aladdin said.
He was outside in the sunlight before he finished the sentence.
He went home and told his mother everything. She was less interested in the adventure than in whether he was injured. He was not. He showed her the lamp. She said it needed polishing. She began to rub it.
What came out of the lamp was not small.
The lamp genie filled the room — immense, powerful, deep-voiced, bowing respectfully. He asked what they wished.
Aladdin's mother fainted.
Aladdin, who had already met one genie today and felt he was getting the hang of it, asked for food.
What arrived on golden trays would have fed a palace. They ate well that night, and sold the golden trays the next day, and the day after that when they were hungry again, Aladdin rubbed the lamp.
He was not idle anymore. Somewhere between the dark cave and the lamp genie, Aladdin had started paying attention to the world. He watched, he listened, he learned the value of things. When he saw the Sultan's daughter through a screen at the market one day and fell completely in love, he didn't just wish for her — he built, slowly and carefully, a version of himself worth knowing.
He asked the genie for gifts to send to the Sultan. He asked for a palace to be built. He asked for a procession. He worked, in his way, for everything he got — not with his hands, but with the steady intelligence of someone who had learned, in a cave in the dark, to think before acting.
He married the princess. He was a good husband and, eventually, a good man.
But the sorcerer came back.
He came back with a cart and a cry: "New lamps for old! New lamps for old!" And the princess, who didn't know the lamp's secret, found the battered old thing in Aladdin's chamber and thought: what a good deal.
She handed it over.
In an instant, the palace — and she — was in Africa.
Aladdin came home to an empty plot of land where his palace used to be. He stood there for a moment. He still had the ring.
He found her. He defeated the sorcerer. He brought her home.
The lamp came with them.
Aladdin was never again the boy in the streets playing games and avoiding his mother's sighing. The cave had changed him, the way dark places sometimes do — they show you what you're made of, and then you have to decide what to do with that information.
He had decided well.

182.title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves
182.text:
His brother Cassim had done considerably better — married a wealthy woman, lived in a fine house, walked around with the air of someone who wanted you to know things were going well. Ali Baba did not hold this against him. He was, by nature, a cheerful man.
One morning, hidden in the branches of a large tree watching forty armed horsemen thunder into the clearing below him, Ali Baba discovered that his life was about to get considerably less simple.
The leader — large, black-bearded, the unmistakable posture of a man accustomed to being obeyed — dismounted and faced a bare cliff wall. He spoke two words.
"Open, Sesame."
The rock split open. The forty thieves carried their heavy saddlebags inside. When they came out, the bags were empty. The leader sealed the rock with two more words and they all rode off as if this were a completely normal way to spend a morning.
"Open, Sesame."
The rock opened.
Inside was a cave large enough to hold a thousand people, and it was full of exactly the kind of things that forty thieves accumulate over a long career: gold coins in open chests, silks, silver plate, jewels, carpets thick enough to lose your hand in. Years of work. Decades, maybe.
The trouble started when his wife wanted to know exactly how much gold there was.
She went next door to borrow a measuring cup from Cassim's wife. Cassim's wife, being curious, put a smear of fat on the bottom before lending it. When it came back, a gold coin was stuck to the fat.
Cassim's wife showed her husband. He confronted Ali Baba immediately.
Cassim went to the forest the next morning, found the cliff, said the magic words, and walked into the cave. He stuffed his bags with as much as he could carry, started for the door, and discovered that in his excitement he had completely forgotten the password.
He tried everything he could think of — wheat, barley, oats, millet, rice, rye.
Not sesame.
He was still trying when the forty thieves returned.
What happened next was not pleasant, and the thieves left Cassim in four pieces as a warning to anyone else who might find their cave.
Morgiana was a slave in Cassim's household. She was also, without question, the cleverest person in this story — which is saying something, because there were forty professional thieves on the other side.
She found a shoemaker in the market, paid him generously, led him to the house blindfolded, and had him repair Cassim's remains so it appeared he had died of natural causes. She organized the funeral so no one asked uncomfortable questions. She solved every problem as it appeared, with the calm efficiency of someone who already knows what they're going to do before they're done hearing the problem.
The thieves' captain figured out that someone had found the cave. He sent a man into the city to investigate. Morgiana met his man at the gate. She offered him coffee. She watched his eyes. She knew immediately.
She danced at the feast that night — a beautiful, whirling dance, a knife flashing in her hand as part of the performance — and at its end, she did what was necessary.
The captain himself came eventually. He came as an oil merchant, with forty jars, one of which held a soldier. He planned to wait until the household was asleep. He had not accounted for Morgiana going to the kitchen late for oil.
She went jar to jar, and at each jar that held more than oil, she acted. All forty. She was methodical. She was efficient. She felt nothing she needed to examine closely about it, because she was protecting her household and that was that.
The captain came down at midnight and discovered that he had lost all his men and was now standing alone in a courtyard in a city that did not like thieves.
He left. He came back later in another disguise, thinking he would try friendship this time. Morgiana recognized him through the disguise. She told Ali Baba. He acted on her information. The problem was permanently resolved.
The cave is still there, somewhere in the hills.
Open Sesame.
But you might want to bring someone like Morgiana along.

183.title: Sinbad and the Valley of Diamonds
183.text:
The porter's name was Hindbad, and he was having a terrible day.
He was carrying a heavy load through the hottest part of Baghdad in the hottest part of the afternoon, his back aching and his feet burning on the stones, when he stopped to rest on the steps of a magnificent house. From inside came the smell of roasting meat and the sound of music and laughter.
Hindbad, sitting in the dust and heat outside, said several things under his breath about the unfairness of a world where some men feasted in marble houses while others broke their backs in the street.
A servant appeared at the door. "My master heard you," the servant said. "He would like you to come inside."
Hindbad assumed this would go badly for him. It did not.
Inside was an elderly man, richly dressed, with a kind face and sharp eyes and the look of someone who had seen things that would make your hair go white. He smiled at Hindbad and gestured at an empty seat at the table.
"I am Sinbad the Sailor," he said. "And you are right that some men feast while others work. Sit down and eat. And let me tell you how this house was paid for. Then you can decide if my fortune came easily."
Hindbad sat. The food was extraordinary. And Sinbad began.
On my second voyage, said Sinbad, I had made one safe journey and come home with good profit, and immediately decided to do it again because the sea gets into your blood and does not come back out. I sailed east with merchants, we stopped at a beautiful green island to rest, I found a soft patch of grass and fell asleep in the shade.
When I woke up, the ship was gone.
I want to be clear about this. Every sailor checks the anchor before going ashore. Every sailor counts heads before departure. These things happened and the ship still left without me. I climbed the tallest tree I could find and looked in every direction.
Water. Just water. No sail anywhere.
I walked to stop myself from sitting down and giving up. In a valley at the center of the island, I found a white dome — enormous, perfectly smooth, no door or window anywhere. I walked around it several times. I could not imagine what it was.
Then a shadow fell over me that turned the whole valley dark.
I looked up. A bird was descending from the sky. A bird so enormous that when it spread its wings, the sun disappeared completely. I had heard stories of the Roc. I had always assumed they were exaggerated.
They were not exaggerated.
The white dome was an egg.
The Roc settled over its egg and tucked its enormous head under one wing, and I thought very quickly. I unwound my turban — a long length of cloth — and tied myself carefully to the Roc's leg. If I was going to get off this island, this was the only way.
At dawn the Roc rose straight into the air, fast and powerful, and I held on with everything I had and did not look down until I was too far up for looking down to be useful information. It flew for a long time and then landed.
I untied myself quickly and looked around.
It had landed in a valley surrounded by cliffs so sheer and so high that there was no way to climb them. The valley floor glittered. I looked down. I was standing on diamonds. Not pebbles of diamond — handfuls. Everywhere. The valley floor was carpeted in them.
I also noticed the snakes. Large snakes. Very large snakes, sleeping in the shade of the cliff walls, each one big enough to swallow a man whole. They came out at night, I would later learn.
It was currently day. That was the good news.
The bad news was the valley walls, which went up and up with no foothold anywhere.
Then something came down from the sky. A large piece of fresh meat, thrown by invisible hands from the top of the cliff. Then another. Then another. This was, I realized, how the diamond merchants of the region collected their gems — they threw down meat, the diamonds stuck to it, eagles swooped down and carried the meat back up to their nests, and the merchants collected the diamonds from the nests.
I had no rope. I had no ladder. I had a great deal of meat falling around me and an excellent turban and a mind that had just spent a very concentrated night thinking about problems.
I tied myself to the largest piece of meat I could find.
An eagle lifted me.
When the merchants at the top found a thin, somewhat traumatized sailor tied to their eagle's dinner instead of diamonds, they were startled. I told them my story. I filled my pockets with diamonds from the valley floor before the eagle had taken me up. I traded those diamonds for passage home.
I came back to Baghdad with enough wealth to build a fine house and never have to worry again.
I immediately planned another voyage.
Hindbad thought about a man tying himself to a piece of raw meat and being carried by an eagle out of a valley of diamonds and snakes.
"No," he said. "I don't think it did."
"Tomorrow," said Sinbad, "I will tell you about the third voyage."
Hindbad came back the next day. He came back every day after that.
He never complained about his load again — or at least, he complained much more quietly.

184.title: The Fisherman and the Genie
184.text:
The fisherman was old, and he was poor, and he went to the sea every morning because that was what he did — had done for forty years — and his wife and children ate what he pulled out of the water.
He cast his net four times a day. That was his rule.
The first cast that morning pulled up mud and stones. The second brought up a broken clay pot. The third came back tangled with old bones and broken glass. He cleaned the net carefully each time, the way you do when patience is the only tool you have.
Fourth cast. He said quietly, "God be with me," which he always said on the fourth cast, and pulled the net in heavy.
It was a jar. Brass, yellowish with age, sealed with a lead cap stamped with a design he didn't recognize. He turned it in his hands. It was clearly very old. It might be worth something.
He pried off the cap with his knife. This took some effort.
Thick smoke poured out — and kept pouring, far more than the jar could possibly hold. It rose and rose and then gathered and shaped itself into something with a face. A face the size of a building. Eyes like coals. The expression of someone who has been waiting a very long time and is not happy about it.
"SOLOMON!" the genie roared. "I repent! I repent! I will never disobey again!"
The fisherman, from the beach, very small and very alone, said: "Solomon has been dead for many centuries. I am just a fisherman."
The genie looked down at him. The genie's anger slowly reorganized itself into something worse: a decision.
"You released me?" the genie said.
"I did."
"Then listen carefully." The genie's voice dropped, which was somehow more frightening than the shouting. "I was imprisoned in that jar as punishment. For the first hundred years, I vowed that whoever freed me would receive great treasures. No one came. For the second hundred years, I vowed three wishes. No one came. By the third century, my gratitude had curdled completely." The genie looked at him with enormous, ancient eyes. "I decided that whoever freed me — I would kill."
The fisherman stood on his beach with his net in his hands.
He was not strong. He had no weapon. He was sixty years old and his back hurt most mornings and he was very obviously not a match for a supernatural being of ancient power.
But he had lived a long time and heard many stories.
"Before I die," the fisherman said, as calmly as he could manage, "I have just one question."
The genie waited.
"That jar." The fisherman tilted his head at it. "I am an old man and I've seen many things, but I cannot picture how someone of your — your size and magnificence — could fit inside something so small. A lesser genie, maybe. But you." He shook his head, almost admiringly. "It seems impossible."
The genie's eyes narrowed. "You doubt me?"
"I mean no disrespect. I simply cannot imagine it."
The genie, who had a great deal of pride and had been sitting in a jar for nine hundred years with no one to tell about it, said: "I will show you."
He contracted. The smoke gathered, swirled, compressed, and poured back into the jar. The fisherman grabbed the lead cap and pressed it down hard.
He sat on the jar.
"Now," the fisherman said to the jar, "hear what I have decided. You told me you planned to kill the person who freed you. Since you have such poor judgment about gratitude, I will throw you back into the sea. You can wait another thousand years for someone else."
The jar, muffled: "Wait."
"I am listening."
A long pause. "If I swear by the name of God to serve you well — will you free me?"
The fisherman thought about it. He was not a cruel man. He was not even particularly angry. He was a practical man who understood that a genie sworn by the name of God was considerably more useful than a genie in a jar.
"Swear," he said.
The genie swore.
The fisherman opened the jar.
What followed was a good partnership. The genie led the fisherman to a lake full of fish of colors he had never seen — red and blue and white and yellow fish, each color a different kingdom's waters, the genie said. He showed him how to cast only once, to the king's palace, and bring up more than his whole family needed.
The fisherman came home that evening with more fish than he had ever caught in a single day. His wife asked what had happened. He told her the whole story.
She was quiet for a moment.
"You sat on the jar," she said.
"I did."
"You, personally. Sat on the genie."
"It seemed like the right move at the time."
She looked at the fish — enough for a week, piled in the yard — and then at her husband, old and thin and entirely pleased with himself.
"It was," she said.
He went back to the lake every day after that. He became, in time, known throughout the city. Not as the man who had tricked a genie — he never told that part to strangers — but as the finest fisherman on the coast, with the best catch, in the worst weather, reliable as the tide.
He cast his net four times a day. He always said "God be with me" on the fourth cast.
He always meant it.

186.title: The Three Princes and Princess Nouronnihar
186.text:
The Sultan had three sons and one problem: all three of them were in love with the same person.
The person was Princess Nouronnihar, his niece, who had grown up in the palace and was, by any measure, worth being in love with. She was clever and kind and the kind of person who made every room better by being in it. The three princes — Houssain, Ali, and Ahmed — were all perfectly good men who each had a reasonable argument for why they were the right choice.
The Sultan could not decide. He thought about it for many nights. Then he called all three before him and announced the solution.
"You will each go out into the world for one year," he said. "Travel wherever you like. Bring back the most extraordinary, the most remarkable, the most useful thing you can find. When you return, I will choose."
The brothers agreed. They walked out of the palace together, down the road, and when it split into three, they embraced, promised to meet back in a year, and went their separate ways.
Prince Houssain, the eldest, traveled east to a great trading city. In the market he found a carpet seller with a plain-looking square of wool. The price was enormous. The property, the seller explained, was this: sit on it, wish to be somewhere, and you'd be there — anywhere in the world, instantly.
Houssain paid without bargaining. He had seen enough to know that useful things rarely look impressive.
Prince Ali, the middle son, went west to a city of craftsmen. In a back alley he found an old man with a carved ivory tube. Look through it, the old man said, and you'd see whatever you wished to see — any person, any place, any event happening right now anywhere on earth. Ali paid the high price and tucked it carefully in his bag.
Prince Ahmed, the youngest, went south through desert and mountains to a market at the edge of a great forest. There he found a single golden apple. The seller explained: any person who smelled this apple, however ill, however close to death, would recover completely.
Ahmed protected that apple like it was made of something more precious than gold.
A year later, the brothers met at the crossroads. They were glad to see each other and swapped stories and admired each other's treasures and were generally pleased with how well the mission had gone.
Then Ali raised his ivory tube and looked through it idly — and stopped.
"Brothers," he said.
They looked at him.
"Look at Nouronnihar."
They each looked through the tube in turn. In the palace far away, Nouronnihar lay surrounded by weeping attendants. She had fallen terribly ill while they were gone. The physicians had tried everything. She was dying.
Houssain didn't need to be asked twice. He pulled out the carpet.
"All three of us. Now."
They sat together. Houssain said the word. The crossroads vanished and the palace appeared around them, and they were kneeling beside Nouronnihar's bed before anyone in the palace had time to notice.
Ahmed held the apple beneath her nose.
Color came back into her face. Her eyes opened. By evening she was sitting up, asking what had happened and why her cousins were all there at once looking like they'd run a very long distance.
The Sultan arrived and was overjoyed and then immediately faced with the original problem, which had not gone away: all three princes had used their treasures to save her, and all three treasures had been necessary. Which one deserved the princess?
The princes debated this themselves. Houssain argued that without the carpet, they would have arrived too late. Ahmed argued that without the apple, she would have died regardless of how quickly they arrived. Ali argued that without the tube, they wouldn't have known she was dying at all.
They were all right. This was the problem.
The Sultan devised an archery contest — the prince who shot the furthest would win. Houssain and Ali shot their arrows great distances. Ahmed shot his and they could not find it at all.
He went to look for it and found it near a pavilion in the hills, beside a woman named Peri Banou who had clearly enchanted it to land there so Ahmed would come looking. She was remarkable in every way, and by the time Ahmed figured out this was a setup, he didn't particularly mind.
He came back to the palace without the princess.
Ali received Princess Nouronnihar as the Sultan had promised.
And Ahmed, who had lost the contest he hadn't really known he was in, went back to the hills to find Peri Banou, which turned out to be a significantly more interesting story than winning a straightforward archery contest.
Houssain, who had genuinely loved Nouronnihar, sat with his grief honestly and then decided to become a hermit and spend his life in contemplation, which suited him better than he expected.
The Sultan kept all three treasures.
He was a practical man.
The lesson, if there is one, is that it is very difficult to judge who deserves credit when everyone contributed something essential. And that sometimes losing what you were competing for leads you somewhere better than winning would have.
Ahmed would have agreed, if anyone had asked him.
He was very happy in those hills.

187.title: The Ebony Horse
187.text:
Three inventors came to the Persian king's festival, each with something extraordinary.
The first brought a golden peacock that spread its tail and marked the hours. The second brought a trumpet that sounded by itself when enemies approached the gates. The third — an old Indian craftsman with quiet eyes and the patient manner of someone who has made one very good thing and knows it — brought a horse.
It was carved from black ebony wood, inlaid with gold and set with jewels, life-size, perfect in every detail. The most beautiful object in the room.
The king asked what it could do.
"This horse," said the craftsman, "can carry a rider anywhere in the world. Through the air, faster than any bird. The peg on the right shoulder sends it upward. The peg on the left brings it down."
The king's son, Prince Firouz Shah, had been watching with the bright eyes of someone who has already made a decision and is just waiting for permission to act on it. Before anyone could discuss further, he stepped forward and asked to try it.
"There is a third control—" the craftsman began.
The prince had already turned the right-hand peg.
The horse rose straight into the air. The court watched, open-mouthed, as prince and horse climbed into the sky, shot forward over the city walls, and vanished.
Everyone turned slowly to look at the craftsman.
"There is a third control?" the king said.
"A small knob inside the horse's mane," the craftsman said carefully. "It must be engaged to descend properly. Without it, the left peg only slows the horse. The rider needs to know to reach inside the mane."
There was a silence.
High above the earth, Firouz Shah was discovering this problem in real time.
He was thrilled at first — the wind rushing past, the earth spread out below in patches of brown and green, the city already small as a game tile. He could see mountains he had only read about. He could see the sea. He tried the left peg.
The horse slowed. It did not descend.
He pushed further. It slowed more. He reversed. It shot upward.
Firouz Shah had been trained well. His tutors had drilled one principle above all: when frightened, think. He worked through the horse's controls systematically, trying each peg in each direction, noting every response. He discovered that the right peg had variable pressure — not just on or off, but degrees. Perhaps the left peg did too.
He searched the carved mane with his fingers, methodically, section by section.
There was a small knob, perfectly smooth, almost impossible to find unless you were looking.
He pressed it. The horse responded. He brought it gently down.
He had landed in a garden of a palace he didn't recognize. It was night — he had traveled far enough to leave the daylight behind. He secured the horse, found the lock mechanism, explored the garden.
He was in Bengal. The garden belonged to the king of Bengal. And in a pavilion at the garden's edge, he found the king's daughter, Princess Shams al-Nahar, asleep in the warm night air.
He should have left. He knew this with perfect clarity. He was a stranger in a foreign kingdom with no invitation and no explanation for how he had arrived, and waking the princess up was not a reasonable next step.
He woke her up anyway.
She was not frightened — she was curious, which told him immediately that she was someone worth talking to. He told her about the horse. She told him about Bengal. They talked until the sun came up, and when he finally left, he had promised to come back.
He came back. Several times. He brought her a gift each time. He was falling in love with the particular carefulness that falls over people when they realize they have found something they don't want to lose.
Her father found out. He objected, strenuously, in the way that kings object to things — which is to say with armed guards.
The guards had not seen the ebony horse.
Firouz Shah put the princess on the horse and turned the right peg.
They arrived in Persia. His father the king was relieved to have his son back and delighted to meet the princess and approximately three seconds away from arranging a wonderful wedding when the old craftsman appeared and complicated everything.
He wanted his payment — the princess, which had been the original deal he had proposed for the horse, before Firouz Shah had launched himself into the sky without hearing the terms. The king, who had forgotten entirely about this arrangement, had to admit the craftsman had a point.
The craftsman did not get the princess. What he got was a dungeon, because the king was also a king and kings have options. The princess went to a comfortable wing of the palace to rest.
The craftsman, from his dungeon, sent word to the king of Bengal that his daughter was in Persia. The king of Bengal arrived with an army, as fathers tend to do in these circumstances.
Firouz Shah put the princess back on the horse, told her exactly how all three controls worked, and suggested she might enjoy a brief flight while the adults sorted out the army situation.
She landed the horse perfectly on the first try. She had paid attention.
The army situation resolved itself when everyone agreed that the prince and princess should simply get married, which had been the obvious solution from approximately the first night they'd talked until sunrise in her father's garden.
The ebony horse stood in the palace courtyard.
Both of them knew how it worked now.
They went on many journeys together, to places neither of them had seen, landing in gardens and on hilltops and once, memorably, on the deck of a ship full of extremely surprised merchants.
The craftsman eventually got a pardon and a considerable amount of money, which was more useful than the princess anyway.
Curiosity, the horse seemed to say, standing in the courtyard with its jeweled eyes and its three controls — curiosity is good. Curiosity without preparation is how you end up very high in the air with no way down.
Know where the third control is before you turn the first peg.

188.title: Ali Cogia, the Merchant of Baghdad
188.text:
He had saved one thousand gold pieces over many years. He wanted to make the pilgrimage to Mecca — the great journey every person of faith hopes to make once in a lifetime — but he couldn't take his savings on the road. Bandits. He needed somewhere safe.
He had a large clay jar in his shop. He put the gold at the bottom, covered it with olives — a jar of olives attracts less interest than a jar of gold — sealed it, and carried it to his neighbor's house.
Hagi Hassan was a man he had known for twenty years. A merchant. Respected. The kind of man you trust with a jar of olives because you have known him for twenty years.
"Keep this for me until I return," Ali Cogia said.
Hagi Hassan agreed without hesitation and stored the jar without giving it another thought.
At home, Hagi Hassan had not thought about the jar in years. Then one evening his wife wanted olives for dinner and the market was closed. He remembered the jar.
He opened it expecting olives past their best.
He found gold.
A thousand pieces of it, under a layer of dried olives.
Hagi Hassan sat on the floor of his shop with a thousand gold pieces around him and had a very long conversation with himself. He was a respected man. He was a trusted neighbor. He had known Ali Cogia for twenty years.
He took the gold. He refilled the jar with fresh olives, resealed it, and put it back exactly where it had been.
He told himself, several times, that if Ali Cogia came back he would simply say he'd known nothing about any gold. He told himself this until it almost felt true.
"Of course!" Hagi Hassan said warmly. "Here it is, exactly as you left it."
"There should be a thousand gold pieces at the bottom," he said.
Hagi Hassan widened his eyes in perfect surprise. "Gold? But you said it was olives! I stored a jar of olives! I know nothing about gold!"
The Cadi was troubled. He could not decide.
But outside the judge's window, some children were playing a game in the street.
One of them — a boy of perhaps ten — had set himself up as a judge, with two other children playing the merchant and the neighbor. He had heard the case being talked about in the market, the way children absorb the conversations of adults, and was now holding his own trial.
The pretend judge called for a jar of olives. He had the pretend merchant describe how old the olives were when the jar was sealed. He had someone taste the olives currently in the jar. He asked how long olives lasted before going bad and whether seven-year-old olives would taste like the fresh ones in the jar.
The real Cadi had been watching from his window. He called the boy inside.
The boy explained his reasoning without being the least bit intimidated, which takes either courage or the particular fearlessness of someone who doesn't fully understand they should be nervous.
The Cadi summoned experts — olive merchants, traders, people who knew their olives — and asked the question the child had asked: could olives in a sealed jar remain fresh for seven years?
They could not.
The olives in the jar were fresh. The olives should have been seven years old.
Hagi Hassan, who had thought he was very clever, had not considered that olives have a memory.
He confessed. He returned the gold. He received the judgment that went with theft and betrayal of trust in a city that took trust seriously.
The boy went back to playing in the street.
The lesson is not only that justice can come from unexpected places. It is also this: the simplest question — asked without agenda, just to understand — can see through the most careful lie.
Children ask simple questions. Adults would do well to keep that habit.
